html:
Stonewall: Roofing DeSoto Parish's Growing Edge Without Pretending It's a City

Stonewall sits on Highway 171 close enough to Shreveport that the last stretch of commuter growth has put more rooftop square footage on the ground than the town had a generation ago. That doesn't mean a commercial district. It means a school campus, a handful of churches, farm-supply and equipment buildings, and a few small offices and clinics serving the surrounding parish, each with its own roof calendar and its own person responsible for it.

A School Campus Carrying Three or Four Roof Ages at Once

DeSoto Parish school buildings out here tend to be additions stacked on additions: an original brick block from decades back, a gym or cafeteria roof from a later bond issue, a portable classroom wing with its own flat roof tied in awkwardly at the parapet. Each section ages on its own schedule, and a facilities director juggling a whole parish's worth of campuses doesn't have time to treat Stonewall's roof as a special case unless something is actively leaking.

We work that reality directly. When we're called out for a ceiling stain in one wing, we look at the transitions to the older sections too, because that's usually where the real problem is sitting: a flashing detail from one construction era that was never properly tied into the next one.

Churches Run Their Own Roof Calendar

A church roof out here is almost always two roofs in one: a steep-slope sanctuary, shingled or standing seam, and a lower-slope fellowship hall or education wing added on later. The person deciding what happens to that roof is usually a trustee or a building committee working off what the congregation can raise that year, not a capital plan on a fixed cycle.

We try to give that committee something they can actually use: a plain description of what's failing, what's cosmetic, and what can wait another budget year without getting worse. A stained ceiling tile in the fellowship hall doesn't automatically mean the sanctuary roof needs attention, and we say so when it's true.

Farm-Supply and Equipment Buildings at the Edge of Town

Move past the school and the churches and most of what's left is metal: pole-barn style buildings behind farm-supply operations, equipment sheds, hay and feed storage. These are screw-down panel roofs more often than standing seam, and the failures are predictable — backed-out fasteners, cracked washers, panel laps that have opened up after enough seasons of expansion and contraction.

None of this needs a wind-uplift study. It needs someone walking the roof, checking fastener condition panel by panel, and re-driving or replacing what's loosened before the next hard rain finds it.

What a Work Order Looks Like When Nobody Here Does Facilities Full Time

The common thread across the school, the churches, and the farm buildings is that roof decisions rest on someone whose actual job is something else — a superintendent, a pastor, a shop foreman. We check for a specific set of things before we ever talk repair versus replace, because that's what actually changes the recommendation:

  • Ceiling stains and their exact location relative to roof penetrations
  • Flashing condition at every roof-to-wall and roof-to-roof transition
  • Fastener and washer condition on any exposed-fastener metal panel
  • Ponding water on any low-slope section after a rain event
  • Gutter and downspout function, especially where trees overhang the roofline

We write that up in plain language, because the person reading it needs to bring it to a school board, a building committee, or a shop owner who wants the short version.

Storm Season on the Highway 171 Corridor

Spring here brings the same severe thunderstorm and hail risk as the rest of north Louisiana, and Gulf-system remnants can dump heavy rain well inland by the time they reach DeSoto Parish. Older shingle roofs on the school's original sections and on sanctuary buildings take the brunt of hail impact, while the metal buildings mostly shrug off wind but can lose fasteners in sustained gusts.

Winter brings its own smaller risk: an ice event a few times a season that most of the roof stock out here was never specifically designed around, since heavy snow and ice loading isn't the default assumption in this part of the state.

Questions Stonewall Building Contacts Ask Us

Do you work on churches, or mostly offices and commercial buildings?

Church roofs are a regular part of what we do out here, and we understand the committee-driven decision process is different from a corporate capital plan.

How do you handle a school roof leak when the district's budget cycle doesn't line up with an active problem?

We separate an emergency dry-in from the permanent fix, so a leak gets stopped now while the larger repair or replacement decision waits for the right budget window.

What's different about maintaining a metal ag building roof versus a standing-seam commercial system?

Exposed-fastener panels need routine fastener and sealant checks that a concealed-fastener standing-seam roof doesn't, so the maintenance rhythm is different even when the material looks similar.

Can a small church or farm building get a real inspection, or is that only for larger properties?

The inspection scope scales to the building. A small roof still gets a full walk and a written account of what we found.

Does hail season change what you'd recommend for a roof out here?

After a significant hail event we recommend a specific check of shingle and metal panel condition before deciding on repair versus replacement, since hail damage isn't always visible from the ground.
